turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 316, Brotherhood of Railway and Steamships Clerks, Freight Handlers, Express and Station Employees, Petitioners versus, Anna Mae Allen et al. Mr. Kramer.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. This case arises from a number of employees of the Southern Railway, suing that Railway and its non-operating unions to enjoin the enforcement of a union-shop agreement entered into by the railroad and the unions. The unions that was sued, the railroad and the union-shop agreements are the identical unions, railroad and agreement that were involved in Street -- in International Association of Machinists against the Street which this Court decided a year and a half ago.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: The AFL-CIO is asking you to reconsider Street, and I think you might well do so. We have not asked the Court to reconsider Street because it is not necessary to do so. The decision below, we say is inconsistent with Street, taking Street as it is. Although, we concur with the AFL-CIO that the Court might well be considerate and state that no cause of action was shown in this case. But taking Street as it is, the plaintiffs in this case have not shown a cause of action under the Street case and even if they had, the relief that was given them is inconsistent with the relief that this Court said could be given when there is a cause of action. In fact, the relief given here is precisely the relief that this Court said could not be given.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: The case -- only inconsistent?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, it is at least inconsistent. It is in direct violation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: The proviso does say, it's directly to the contrary.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: It is directly contrary. The plaintiffs below chose not to comply with the union-shop agreement. Instead, they sued to enjoin its enforcement saying that they were not members of the union, did not pay it any money, did not want to pay it any money, and that to enforce the agreement against them would be unlawful. Now after this Court decided the Hanson case that was a case that came from Nebraska in which this Court upheld the validity of the union-shop agreement and reversed the Supreme Court of Nebraska which had set it aside on the ground that the money might or might not be used in accordance with the individual's view. This Court in this Hanson case said that wasn't enough; might or might not and therefore, reversed. This is exactly the Hanson case. The case was tried before a jury. 14 of the plaintiffs testified and their counsel stated that of the others, two were no longer employees, one was in a position exempt from the union-shop agreement, and that the remainder if they testify, would testify the same way that the 14 did.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: This is not a class action?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And this was brought as a class action but the trial court did not sustain that it's a class action.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That is correct Mr. Justice. Now, all the plaintiffs who testified stated that they were not members of the union and were opposed to union-shop. Now, we have some -- their testimony is summarized on pages 7 to 11 of my brief. All that they testify to was that they were opposed to a union-shop and that they were opposed to unions engaging in political activities. Not one of them, although asked, could name a single political cause supported by the unions contrary to their views. None of them testified that he or she had advised the union of his views or indeed had advised the union of anything.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Was there any showing that the union had publicized the political causes and candidates of -- which it had supported?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: There was not.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So how did they know? How could the plaintiffs know?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: These plaintiffs aren't even members of the union. Now what the union does is not a secret. They could ask. The union has publications and under the Street case, this Court held that the individual must let the union know what he opposes. In this case, he couldn't even name anything that the union did that it opposed and was asked, name a single candidate that the union supported to whom you're opposed and none of them could do it. Name a single piece of legislation that the union supported to which you are opposed and none of them could do it except that two of them said, “He was opposed to the enactment of Section 2 Eleventh of the Railway Labor Act.”

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, and a couple of others or more referred to the state right to work (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: But they didn't know whether the union supported or opposed it.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Oh, was there any way they could have found out?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: They could have asked. Well first, fundamentally under the Street case, these people haven't paid any money to the union. The union wasn't spending their money whatever it was doing. And this Court held that it's the spending of their money for causes they opposed which is unlawful, but they haven't paid anything.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They will lose their position if they didn't know [Inaudible].

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well that's right. But until they have paid some money, they have no grievance. Until the union does something with their money contrary to their views then under the Street case, they have no act -- cause of action. This Court said specifically that the union-shop is lawful and that the plaintiffs in the Street case said, “The plaintiffs therefore remain obliged to pay the sums hold for by the union-shop agreement,” in practically those words, that they remain obliged to pay and the opinion of the Court that was stated that there was nothing unlawful about the union-shop, there was nothing unlawful about collecting the money. If there is any illegality involved, the illegality would be spending their money contrary to their views. That was explicit in Mr. Justice Brennan's opinion. Well, the other one --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Will they have to wait -- they'd -- they have to wait until the money has been spent before they can check?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well I have to wait until they paid it. And then they may seek to have it spent for purposes which they opposed.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: No, not to which they opposed.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: They may seek to prevent this expenditure for purposes which they opposed. Now, the other evidence in this case --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: Suppose the evidence [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: If they had an agreement?

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: No, if when a -- the union must -- they have an agreement [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well that, of course, would be illegal that the union gave money to cope.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: But they gave any [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: They have to wait before they take the money although the union had that understanding (Inaudible)?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: They could say under the Street case -- I'm just arguing this under the Street case, not the way I argued the Street case, but assuming the Street case to pronounce the law as it is today, they could either insist -- they could insist either on the union not spending a portion of their money force up to contribution, or, I think the alternative was that they could be relieved of a portion of the dues -- the Court indicated those two alternatives.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: You're in effect could object those [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: None of these unions have. I --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Some of the unions have?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: In this industry, I think only one union does. The Brotherhood of Railroad Trainmen, I think, is the only one that had a check off agreement and they're not in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: How about [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, very common outside the railroad industry. It is lawful under the Railway Labor Act but the unions just haven't been able to get it. Now the other evidence in this case consists of a showing that the AFL-CIO engages in certain legislative and other activities. There is no evidence that those activities of the AFL-CIO with which these unions are affiliated and to which it makes payments, there is no evidence that anything the AFL-CIO does is contrary to the views of any plaintiff.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Does the -- the primary [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, very extensive.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: It relates to precisely where the money went and how much is spent, was there?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No. It just says substantial amounts. The precise amounts were not stipulated.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: If it was a stipulation, they [Inaudible] --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes, that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is that it?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. And it did say there contrary to the views of the plaintiff, there was no evidence of anything that any of the unions do or anything that the AFL-CIO does is contrary to their views.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, they testified as I understand it that that was contrary to their views to have the Brotherhood spend any money for political purposes.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. One of these --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: They -- if they wanted their money spent for political purposes, they wanted to spend it themselves for the --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- for the causes in candidates in which they believe. They didn't want --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right and under --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- somebody taking their money away from them which is -- it was necessary for them to pay out of the statute in order to keep their jobs and then spending that money for causes and candidates of a political nature at all. That's what they're opposed to, isn't what the record shows?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. And under the Street case, and under the Hanson case, both, that is not enough. That is exactly what was shown in the Hanson case. That is exactly what was shown in Lathrop against Donohue.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: No, no, in the Hanson case, as you told us this morning, it was -- the record there was that the money might or might not be spent for political --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh no, it would be spent for political purposes which might or might now coincide with their views.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Because they are saying, “You're not opposed to any [Inaudible]?”

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: At all, exactly.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Even if they come in and say, use that 35% of your income [Inaudible]?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Under both the Street case and the Hanson case. That was exactly what the Hanson case was. It was shown that they do spend money for political purposes, but all that was shown and all the Supreme Court of Nebraska found was that those purposes might or might not coincide with the plaintiffs' views. That was -- this is exactly the Street v. Hanson case and this is exactly Lathrop against Donohue which was --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So, your position is that the [Inaudible]?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. No one has such a cause of action and that's what this Court held in the Street case and in the Hanson case that they must show support of a specific cause, of a specific candidate which they opposed and off which they notified the union they opposed before they have a cause of action. And then when they have a cause of action, they cannot get an injunction against the collection of all money which is exactly the relief given in this case. This union-shop agreement has been in effect with over 10 years now and these people haven't paid a dime under a preliminary injunction and the permanent injunction. Now, when this case was appealed to the Supreme Court of North Carolina, first, that court reversed by a 6 to 1 vote. But after the decision of the trial court and what later became the Street case, they granted a rehearing and our rehearing divided evenly and said the even division does not leave in effect their first opinion but leaves in effect the trial court's opinion. Oh, one other thing -- one other --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: It is in effect today.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, I think so.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Mr. Justice, I don't have information that remains in effect in the Street case too, today.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Why do you say that?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I know, I had to locate, it's in effect today.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I know. I asked this Court to a writ of mandamus which the Court denied me, but the Street case today, the injunctions in effect. That was not the mandate of this Court. And this injunction is in effect today, although the language is explicit in the Street opinion that an injunction against the collection of all funds is not a proper remedy, -- that's exactly those words. It's not a remedy appropriate to. The -- I had the words in the reversed order. Now, the only other evidence to support their cause of action is that the -- is at COPE, The Community on Political Education of the AFL-CIO and railway labors politically makes contributions to campaigns of particular candidates. The uncontradicted evidence not a word to the contrary is that every cent of that comes from voluntary contributions and not one cent comes from dues. In addition, there was no evidence that any candidate supported by COPE or RLPL was opposed by any of the plaintiffs.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, I'll ask you first as to your first point that the jury found otherwise.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Looking as page 22, the first -- the first [Inaudible] for example.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: The jury found -- the jury found that the unions contribute --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Dues and fees which they collect from railroad employees --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- in support of or opposition to legislation and that's a -- you see -- say that there's --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: But they're not talking about RLPL and COPE.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: No, they're talking about the Union.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. And of course, there's not a word of evidence to that effect.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, did you file a motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well you do have jury findings contrary to what you're telling us.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right, there's not a word in the record to support that because -- and there wasn't any point --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, -- certainly, because none of the money that is paid to the AFL -- AFL-CIO by the unions goes to COPE for political expenditures, none of it. The evidence is uncontradicted that every cent of the money that they pay comes from voluntary contributions, because it would be a violation of the Corrupt Practices Act or otherwise, in all probability. COPE and RLPL were established after the Corrupt Practices Act was amended which prevented unions for making contributions to campaigns of candidates for federal office.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: It would not and perhaps the jury had contributions to State candidates in mind. But more significantly, none of the plaintiffs said that he was opposed to any candidate that COPE or RLPL supported.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They did say they were opposed to having their money used for political purposes, didn't they?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: For political purposes at all regardless of what they were.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But your point is that in any event there was no evidence that anything [Inaudible] where in fact [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No, there is not one word of evidence and indeed with respect to COPE and RLPL there is a stipulation that none of the -- none of those contributions came from either of these unions.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Was there such a stipulation in reference to the finding number two of the jury?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I don't recall what's number two --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Does an independent union used dues and funds which they collect from railroad employees influenced vote in elections to public officers, the answer, “Yes.” You challenge that --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: To influence votes? No, I would, I would not challenge that they tried to influence votes. I do challenge that they made contributions to campaigns.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, what do you mean by that --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, they have public --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's what I'm talking about, yes. That -- in their other periodicals, they sometimes announce the candidates they support and urge their members then the --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Of course, that's the -- dues money is used for that.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, I -- Mr. Justice White, many people do. I distinguish between political and legislative.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Don't you think -- you want to call [Inaudible]?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, I call it legislative activity. It can be called political but the unions are --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No, its legislative when you're before a legislative body.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible] submitted this for [Inaudible] candidate?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Then that's it.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So, that is a [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, for political, I haven't questioned that there was evidence that they used money for political purposes. I challenge that they contribute money to campaigns.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well what about number four? It says that the defendant unions used dues and fees that they collect from railroad employees to make contributions to the campaigns of candidates for election to public office.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: There is not a word of evidence to support that. But even if there were, Mr. Justice Black --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But it was that challenge that (Inaudible) --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: We asked the judge not to submit such a question.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well after he did submit it, did you move to set it aside?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: On the ground that was not supported by evidence?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No, we moved to --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So you didn't?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: On the ground that it was not supported by evidence? All the argument took place before it was submitted. Before it was submitted the Court -- we argued before the judge that there was -- there's no evidence on that.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, then you had a finding by the jury that there was -- that these facts did occur?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I -- yes, but you can't find a word of evidence in the records to support a contribution to a campaign, except these long lists of contributions by COPE and RLPL --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: What? I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: That they paid contributions in the campaign?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Page 22.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: 22, item 4.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible] used dues and fees as such?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And you did not present as one of the questions presented that you -- the question of whether the findings were supported by evidence?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Not -- not in this Court because it isn't necessary because for two reasons, none of these dues or -- no dues or fees came from any of these plaintiffs. There is no evidence that the plaintiffs object to any of the candidates the union supported if they supported them, whether through contributions or otherwise.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, have they paid any dues and fees?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: They have not paid one cent.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They've been going to keep it from -- keep you from collecting them --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right, we haven't --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's the reason.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's the reason there is but that doesn't -- that doesn't answer the problem, does it? They enjoined you to keep you from collecting them on the ground that they didn't want them used for political purposes?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You say they do not have standing to raise that that way?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. I say they don't have no standing until certain things happened. One, that they have paid some money.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But they -- they come in and say we don't want to pay it because it's illegal and we don't want to pay it and the Court enjoins.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: But in Street, this Court specifically --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Your point -- your point is that Street, we held could not have enjoined the payment of the dues under the union establishment?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: If the Court decided anything in that case they certainly decided that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is that the part that you are asking us to overrule?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, no. I'm asking you to overrule that there was a course of action at all. Your Honor, even in your opinion, even in, even in your partially dissenting opinion, you repeatedly stated that the objection must be to a specific cause and he must notify the union what it is and because you said --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well this -- according to this as far as specific cause they don't want their money used for political purposes.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, you spoke about specific candidates --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I didn't say that they had to. I -- the idea that I was -- I'm sorry of it?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If I indicated there's a difference between making a man -- freeing a man from having his money extracted from him to support one candidate and extracted from him to support a group of candidates in political causes.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, you certainly, repeatedly said a particular candidate or a particular cause and in addition you repeatedly said --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That wouldn't be one of the reasons that for a particular candidate or particular cause.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: But I -- it --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: My thought -- my (Inaudible) -- my objection was on the ground that the union didn't have a right have the -- half of the law to force people to pay dues to it and it's going to be used for political purposes --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: To which --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- one way or the other.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, no. But you specifically said to which they object, the cause to which they object --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's what they object.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And -- but a particular cause you said and of which --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I didn't mean by that, that they had to go and look at each particular candidate to see whether that was the particular one.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And you also --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That would put a burden, I never would have thought of putting on you.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: You also said they have to notify the union of the cause to which they object.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well they did by enjoining them, didn't they?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, they haven't notified them of any particular cause of any particular candidate.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You said the objective, their money being used for political purposes.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes, and they didn't say that, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And that's by all political causes, all political causes?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And all legislative activity.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: They have in every single political cause --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: All. If they -- they couldn't engage in any political or legislative activity and as Your Honors know, I'm sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: No, it is -- there's no implication they took their money.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: But they haven't given them any money.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible] you still have to pay [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. That it would have to be a particular cause and also that they must notify the union what it is. Even in your opinion, Mr. Justice Black, you repeatedly said, “Of which they have notified the union.”

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I meant as I gather this, they say they have notified them.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: They did not notify --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They notified them by an injunction and got an injunction to keep them from still using their money for that purpose.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, this is an -- this is a theory of all law I find difficult to understand.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I find it difficult to understand why you don't argue it on the merits.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And you argued to speak --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: There's a difference -- there's a difference, a vital difference between those who think they have a right to spend their money for political purposes by forcing it from them and those who think if they have all the right in the world if they've take up contribution, that's your issue.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well this is the difference.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I just -- I don't want to --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No, it --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I don't want to get to a controversy with my Brother Black, but if I understand your position you don't have to reach any of these -- any question in this case beyond [Inaudible] of the law they get on to be overruled [Inaudible] still law. What Street held was that you just can't bring an action like this into the type of money.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. Your --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Oh, if that's so, the case is over.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible] case overruled.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right and Mr. Justice Black, Mr. Blakeney is going to make an argument similar to what you're suggesting and that is that they notified them by suing them. Well, this isn't a peculiar --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I mean, -- maybe the Court did hold that, I didn't so read his opinion.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No, I thought that they --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You just said that you couldn't notify him by -- and then file a lawsuit, enjoin it and if that would --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: But -- I find it --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- keep it from doing anything.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I find it difficult to understand how somebody -- let's say he needs -- he has to notify him to have a cause of action. So somebody who doesn't have a cause of action against somebody else acquires a cause of action against that somebody else by suing him. Now that's a theory for -- a document of jurisprudence I can't grasp.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But I suggest -- you assume what you're arguing on the merits [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I have about one minute left, Mr. Justice White. I think -- oh, no, you started five minutes late. I have about six minutes left. The merits of course were fully argued in the Street case and the Court divided five ways. Basically, it is if that all -- well, there were several propositions. First, there is no government action here. All that Congress has done is to repeal its own prohibition against the union-shop, and secondly, to say that state law shall not apply to union-shop agreements in the railroad industry. And that's all its -- that -- that's all the federal action there is. Now there were those who say superseding state law makes it federal action and therefore the unions -- in getting a union-shop agreement are bound by constitutional limitation. I think that's wrong. I think that repealing your own law, repealing your own prohibition is clearly constitutional and superseding state law and what is the most essential of interstate activities, the railroad industry is not unconstitutional. And so therefore, it shouldn't reach any constitutional question but if we do reach the constitutional questions, then I think this act -- this conduct is lawful. There is no right of the plaintiffs that is being in no constitutional right being infringed, I know Mr. Justice Black disagrees with me.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And Mr. --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, I certainly do. I think that was the only fully sound opinion of the five. I think the opinion of Justice Frankfurter in which Mr. Justice Harlan concurred was the sound view and expresses the view that I would now expound that no rights are being infringed because these people are not being prevented from saying anything or writing anything or supporting or opposing anything. They are not being ordered or made to say anything except in the very indirect sense to which Mr. Justice Black referred in his opinion that using their money to publicize certain ideas this in effect making them say it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Isn't it?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No. I think not. I think it is not any more, Your Honor -- well, I think -- one of the examples I gave in the Street case was that, the Federal Government certainly takes tax money that is exacted and it uses some of that money to employ [Inaudible] of various states which preached their faiths.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What would you say if the Federal Government took their money to support the Republican Party, your tax money, how do you feel about it?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, the suggestion has been made.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And maybe -- maybe I'll better put in the Democrat.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No. Yes, in my case make it a Democrat. I would object, but I don't think I would have any constitutional right. The suggestion has seriously been made that the Federal Government financed the presidential campaigns.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That‘s right, the presidential campaigns but that's quite human and that's what -- the Federal Government doing something in connection with regulating election.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That‘s right. But --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But your argument is that a man who wants a job, the only way he can get it by -- a reasonable closed shop is to join the union or pay dues. They -- the union then as to the Federal Governments say, you have to do that and take his money and spend it for political purposes when he is supposed to.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Of course the Federal Government didn't say he has to join the -- it has to join the union.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: No, but they give you -- they fix the power that way so that when you come -- turns out and spend it he has to if he gets his job.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, if the union is successful in negotiating --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes, that's right.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: -- union-shop agreement and it doesn't even have -- doesn't have them all over yet. So --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: No, it doesn't have them all.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Does in this case --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It does in this case.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And does in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That's what we're talking about.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: But it's not because of the Federal Government except in the sense that the Federal Government repealed this prohibition.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And I would submit, Mr. Justice White, that it didn't have to preempt. I would suggest that the state laws couldn't have --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible] they did have to be [Inaudible] held what it did?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: It‘s been held that it preempted in so many words, it has not been held that it had to.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, it's not a federal contract except --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No, the fede -- the -- all that federal law does with effect to that contract has put certain limitations on it.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Do you say that -- would you say that the union-shop is an agreement [Inaudible]?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: The limitations are controlled.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right and I would suggest the state law couldn't prevent it anyway because it would interfere with interstate commerce to have a union-shop agreement, say, on the Southern Railroad with subways in 13 states legal in some states and illegal in others. But the Supremacy Clause of the Constitution is an article of the Constitution. When Congress exercises a power specifically conferred on it by the Constitution, it cannot be acting unconstitutionally.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Mr. Kramer, [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That's right and that was a com -- that was very common before we had the federal legislation on the subject, very common.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: It does not show up yet --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Three of them had. It was a -- it's a silly situation. The injunction is still in effect as to all six who put up a bond. Three have always been members; one is retired and one is in an accepted position not subject to the union-shop. But the injunction is after all six of them, three of them have all -- have been members all along.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible] Do you think that an injunction restraining [Inaudible]?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I could stop with that, Mr. Justice Goldberg, so far as the remedy is concerned. I would not want the Court to stop with that. I want the Court to say that under Street case, these people have not proven a cause of action so they for -- for both reasons. First, they haven‘t proven a cause of action, and secondly, even if they have, the remedy was the wrong remedy.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well you, you speak as though the only remedy was that paragraph which appears at the top of page 23 in the record. Part of the remedy is -- and a very important part of it is the following paragraph, is it not? Street had nothing to do with the -- it didn't concern itself with this kind of a remedy.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: That proviso you're talking about?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Provided however as of today, the unions are completely enjoined from collecting anything from these plaintiffs. They can't collect anything. Now there is a proviso that if the unions come back and prove what portion of their dues they spend for collective bargaining, plus collective bargaining, then that portion of their dues with respect to all of them, with collection of that portion would not be enjoined. Mr. Blakeney will argue that that means they are not enjoined, but they are today enjoined and have been for 10 years enjoined from collecting anything and if only if they come back and make certain showings that injunction may or may not be modified, and that showing is -- would excuse these people from paying anything for anything other than collective bargaining.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It's not a matter of modifying the injunction at the -- this is a very term of the decree?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes, it was not applied to such portion as they proved to the Court is used only for collective bargaining. In other words, if the unions go to Congress --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, it will be reasonably necessary and related to collective bargaining --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- between the defendant unions --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- and the plaintiff's part.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: They couldn't go to Congress under that and as to an increase and the benefits of the -- under the Railroad Unemployment Insurance Act or under the Railroad Retirement Act.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose you're -- suppose you're going to the Court and say we've decided that we're not going to try to force people to pay dues in the long run which will be used for collect -- for supporting candidates or political causes, would this injunction still work against him?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: It would.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Have you asked him to change it on that basis?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I have not asked him to change it, I appealed it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But you didn't want that? You have -- you‘re not ready to say that you will not use money collected from them, force them to pay it and then use it for political purpose.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Because we never could agree on whatever their political purpose is. No, I've -- I've not asked for that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I think both of us could agree that when you spend money to support candidates, political purposes --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- or parties?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I'm not sure we would agree whether seeking an amendment of the Railroad Unemployment Insurance Act is a political purpose.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, that's not -- that -- I doubt if you've had any trouble with the Court on that.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Well, we certainly have because the Court has said we could claim --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But your main trouble is, isn't it -- why not face it, that your main trouble is that you think if we were wrong in holding that the union which has a close shop is not free to force its people to pay dues to use for political purposes.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I -- I didn't --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Ordinary everyday political purposes like supporting parties or candidates.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I think you were wrong in that respect, yes. I think that the opinion of Mr. Justice Frankfurter, Mr. Justice Harlan was right that that is lawful and that -- but the Courts says -- I'm not asking the Court now to reconsider it. I'd be happy to have the Court reconsider it and set a time for argument because I think it was wrong when it went that far, but to decide this case you don't have to go that far. In this case, under the Street case, under its unequivocal language, they have not proven a cause of action and the relief granted is expressly contrary to the relief which the Street case said could be given when there is a cause of action.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Just before you sit down, Mr. Kramer, I have a question. In Great Britain, as I understand it, there is a specific provision that employees who are under like the bargaining agreements like this can “contract out” as they call it there. Now, they had trouble in defining what is and what is not political activity?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: No, the contracting out there -- you see in Great Britain, one of the political party is this --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Is the Labor Party.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Is the Labor Party itself.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Now, they use the money to support their own candidates and they have different rates of dues for those who do not want to have their money used for political purposes, but that's by statute.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I'm just asking you that -- how -- whether it's difficult or has been -- has proven difficult to do --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Oh, but that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- doing it? How about the lobbying activities?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I know of no trouble about lobbying activities. Unions are not restricted to lobbying activities.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: In Great Britain --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: What --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I'm talking about Great Britain.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes, in Great Britain, because they are not restricted because they don't need lobbyist. The members of the legislature themselves are their lobbyists. They don't have much in the way up lobbying except by the members of the House itself. And incidentally, Mr. Justice Stewart, it would --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It's not a comparable situation (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Not a comparable situation.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- your point?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: And incidentally, the amount that's contracted out is about -- is usually one shilling per year, sometimes two shillings per year.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: Are you going to say anything about the welfare funds and the insurance funds?

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: I'd like to but I see two lights here. I --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You have a moment to do it.

turns.name: MILTON KRAMER
turns.text: Yes. Another one of the things that you -- that that -- in other words, the grounds of the injunction is that one of these two unions, all unions but two were dismissed from the case because none of the plaintiffs was represented by any of the others. One of these unions had a death benefit fund and they are -- the unions could not collect anything from the plaintiffs, so long as they have this death benefit fund. Now, this death benefit fund is a fund that varies in amount depending on how long a person has been a member. And when he dies, his wife or estate, is given the amount of -- it goes up as high as $250. Now it also -- it isn't just how long you've been a member, it's how long you have continuously been a member and so it serves two functions. First, it serves a function of having some quick money readily available for the family of a railroad employee who dies and secondly, it reduces the expenses of collecting dues because there's an inducement for people to keep their dues current because the longer they are continuously been a member, the more their death benefit will be. Now it is held that this is an improper expenditure of union dues under a union-shop. These are things that unions have done since time immemorial. In fact, some of the unions were organized with a very first as paternal beneficial associations to have death benefits for the families of employees who died. There was a reason that some of the unions were created and they only at later engaged in collective bargaining. This held that this is not a proper union activity for people who don't want to belong to the union. This is something unions have done and since they were established, it is not taking away; it's not depriving anybody of any constitutional right. Nobody said he objected to death benefits. It was put to them in terms of, do you want to be ordered to take insurance with so and so -- that company and they said, “No, I'd like to pick my own company to buy insurance.” That's the only evidence that this is opposed by any of the plaintiffs. None of them said they were opposed to union paying death benefits to the families of deceased members. And if any of them did -- if any of them felt that way, none of them advised the union that he didn't want his money spent that way and he couldn't advice the union that he doesn't want his money spent that way because he hasn't given any -- the union any money to spend.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Blakeney.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: -- may it please the Court. I would first like to summarize briefly, if I may, just what it is that the record in this case establishes. It's clear first that these plaintiff employees have been notified by the defendant unions and by their employer, the Railway Company that they will be discharged from their jobs of long standing unless they pay money to the defendant unions. It's further established that this money will, in part, be used for political purposes unrelated to collective bargaining. The plaintiff employees have gone upon the witness stand and have stated that they are deeply opposed to money being taken away from them by compulsion against their will and used for political purposes as to which they have no opportunity of choice or decision. Now this, we submit to this Court, is a double wrong of major import, the taking of money from people against their will as a condition of working and the using of that money to put men into the offices of Government.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible] is this, one candidate says, “I believe it is compulsory arbitrations upon the railroads, the only way you could [Inaudible] -- that have a political interest. Now, another candidate says, “I believe in free collective bargaining.” Is that the political purpose under [Inaudible] collective bargaining in that campaign?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I must admit, Mr. Justice Goldberg, that there is of course often difficult question as to what is political in nature. However, this Court in the Street case used the phrase political purposes throughout and certainly to contribute money directly to the campaign of a candidate to get him elected to office is a political purpose and it is established in this case that that is what is done with this money.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: What was your answer be directly to the question that I have put?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: If one candidate is [Inaudible]

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Let's assume [Inaudible] campaign was [Inaudible] arbitration was the -- voluntary arbitration to your collective bargaining.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: And one candidate espoused it and --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: One candidate espoused that and [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: My position, sir, would be that if the unions took money involuntarily from the employees and said, “We are going to put your money on this candidate, not that candidate.” And the employee has no opportunity to dis -- to express or decide which candidate he wants it on and the union officials put it on one, that is an expenditure of political purposes condemned by the Street decision.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Supposed I'd say ma -- Suppose -- oh, pardon me, pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I'm just going to say, suppose that same issue was before the Congress and some money was spent to support the free bargaining, would that be political?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Not in electing a Senator or a --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: No, but no.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: -- or Congressman.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Appearing before the Congress and advocating free and collective bargaining as opposed to compulsory arbitration.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: This would be a closer question, I think, Your Honor. A closer question and might have to be as Mr. Justice Frankfurter once expressed it, decided by litigated elucidation. But at any rate sir, there are unquestionably in this case expenditures undeniably political, undeniably so. The jury --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But I suppose that this one man -- this man who said, I will vote this way on this one issue. He's running for an office, there's many other things, he shouldn't have -- doesn't express himself on that, the --

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: That's true, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And the man might be against -- I've had that extreme that of myself and realize it, a man might be -- for a man's expression of views on labor one way or the other and yet be against him strongly.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Indeed, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE HARLAN
turns.text: I have the experience that.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Indeed, sir, but the point is that the jury found and it is established here that that money was used to elect certain candidates to influence elect -- influence elections to public office and also to effect certain legislation and defeat other legislation and at least some of that is political purposes. That I think it cannot be denied. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I always have and I do when I was engaged in political -- in a campaign for office, I was engaged in what most people thought about as a political campaign.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: That certainly the general idea, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, sir. Now, I was saying that to take the money by compulsion, to use it for such purposes, is a major erosion both of individual liberty and a free government, and it is therefore indeed an import of area.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, you – [Inaudible] the injunction is [Inaudible] an injunction against -- based on such a party, an injunction by way of remedy against the payment of dues. What do you do with what we said in Street about that?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I recognize that I must come clearly to that and I will in detail, Your Honor, but at this moment may I simply say there is indeed language in Street that looks in the direction of refusing such injunction as we have here. There is other language in Street that looks in favor of what we have here and most important of all, Your Honor, each and every defect that you pointed to for reversing the Street judgment is corrected in this judgment, each as I will try to show. Now, the jury in this case and has already been noted, this case unlike Street, was not litigated upon stipulations of evidence but upon the presentation of evidence and testimonies subject to examination and cross examination in the ordinary manner. And the issues therefore were decided not by the Court but by a representative jury upon issues submitted to them by ordinary procedure. I've mentioned that merely in passing. The evidence consisted of both testimony and the exhibits. The exhibits for example, Your Honors, included many pages setting forth by date, by name, by amounts, hundreds of thousands of dollars contributed by agencies of the unions to political candidates. And it includes for instance --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: By these petitioners directly to the candidates, the evidence does not so show rather it --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: For publications, indeed sir for candidates through other agencies and that's what I was about to call your attention to in response to your question. Through these unions, put money into the AFL-CIO which in turn put money into COPE, the Committee for Political Education so called. These unions also put money into --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What you're saying is -- are you sure you're talking about money and either these petitioners or the AFL-CIO is not dues?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: All through dues I'm talking about, Your Honor, compulsory dues, nothing else.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Mr. Blakeney, you're talking right about the capital contributions, these railroad contributions made to the AFL-CIO for their affiliations.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I am sir, so far as AFL-CIO is concerned which AFL-CIO in part took and gave to COPE which COPE in turn took and gave to political candidates. And also to RLPL through RLEA which are railway labor organizations, Railway Labor Executive's Association, Railway Labor's Political League, likewise made these contributions these exhibits here in the back of my brief, lists these expenditures by RLPL.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: They were except that my adversaries still contend, as Your Honors note, not formally because they are too late to contend it formally. It's settled by a jury verdict and by lower court --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible] that's direct contribution to a candidate.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, sir, pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: That's true, sir, but the jury filed against him on that. He's at war with the record. Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Was that [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: The other admits he did pay money to print its publications for political purposes. I mean, direct out of his treasury, direct. But the candidates for political office got it, so the jury found through these other agencies and believe me it was the most difficult matter to trace. And -- but all that is resolved by the trial, if the Court please, he argued to the judge before it went to the jury that the evidence wouldn't sustain such a finding. After the jury -- he argued to the jury they shouldn't find it after the jury verdict he contented it should be set aside. He argued then to the Supreme Court of North Carolina that the evidence didn't sustain it. All those courts went against him on that subject. All those tribunals went against him and that's settled. He cannot herein this Court, I take it, argued that the evidence doesn't sustain the verdict. Rather we've got two questions before us now as I understand. His first point is that our cause of action is defeated any how, no matter the finding, defeated because we have insufficiently advised the unions as to what causes our candidates they espoused and we opposed. Now, the first answer to that, Your Honors is that in Street, the plaintiffs were found to have a valid cause of action and they made known their opposition in no different sense than what these plaintiffs here have. Furthermore, if the Court please as Mr. Justice Stewart mentioned, how can these plaintiffs, how could they have made known their opposition any more specifically because the record is replete that these unions don't even notify their own local officials as to who they support and what causes they support and these local officials testified. Again and again, I don't know what they do with the money, whom they support and whom they oppose, how then could our class, adversary litigants have known? And what must we do about notifying them other than as this Court said in a Street, through this litigation let them know that we oppose and they went on the stand and they said, “We oppose”, we've got their testimony quoted considerably in our brief and I submit it is pointed, positive, repeated and rather eloquent. They say, when we -- when money of ours is used to elect people to government, we think we ought to say, “Do we want our money to go to this man or to that man,” and not somebody else make that decision for us.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Mr. Blakeney, are you relying solely here to have it on political grounds the abandonment that you present -- which you made below unless there are [Inaudible] to the jury findings that upon the -- these contributions for legislative purposes [Inaudible] the collective bargain and used the money [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No, sir. I contend that --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Could you say a few words about that, in efficient to political matters with justifications.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I don't know that those can be excluded from being political matters. I don't know that the -- that the pushing for a legislation is not a political matter. The jury found --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: I thought you [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No sir, no sir. I say that I don't try to define what all is political matter.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Under the special powers [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: The jury means special findings [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: And those special findings were made with three or four subjects. One is the opposition for legislation.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Now, you would say that that's political and unrelated [Inaudible].

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I do now so contend. Yes, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: What if the legislation for a full proof [Inaudible] related to collective bargaining in your State?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I do not -- to seek to pass the law on that it seems to me would not -- would be political matters. Now I can --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible] which is passed on some of them [Inaudible] definitely to collective bargaining?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I do not know that that could be said to be unrelated to collective bargaining. The jury found, Your Honor, not about this and that hypothetical case, they said its been shown in this case to us that this unions take this money and they do seek legislation which is unrelated to collective bargaining and this Court said the same in Street.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: But your position [Inaudible].

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Your position would be, I take it, the question that I asked you was the -- would you [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I do not say that, sir necessarily --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Do you --

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I say that --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I did not, sir. That particular legislation was not up in this case.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Is the [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No, sir. They use it as an argument just as Your Honor is now suggesting. The jury said in effect their verdict dismissed certainly it is, whatever may be cases of particular pieces of legislation whether they are political or not at any rate in this case there has been shown matters that are political, that money is being used for and these matters that have been shown to us are unrelated to collective bargaining and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose they are related to collective bargaining, are you saying that because political matters' legislations are related to political -- collective bargaining, are you agreeing that that couldn't be political?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No sir, I'm --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible] political part, we've had in this country from the beginning have been over that issue.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I do not say that, sir. All I say is what this Court said in Street. I merely use this, as far as this Court win. This Court did go so far as to say that political matters that are unrelated to collective bargaining, it cannot take money from a man and spend it on and against his will. Now, we're --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose -- suppose this law was one which provided that members of the Chamber of Commerce couldn't get a job unless he paid dues to the courts of Chamber of Commerce dues, there's a lot of members of the Chamber of Commerce [Inaudible]. What would you say about that law?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I think it'd be political.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Now, if the Court please though --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Exactly what's political about death benefits?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: About death benefits? Death benefit is a corner of this case as a case that has never been much argued or discussed. Then I rest nothing upon that sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible] as the basis to justify the injunction?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: If this Court should come to the conclusion that that portion of the ca -- of the judgment should be eliminated, I say that it can be eliminated without any effect upon the real thrust of this judgment. Now may I come quickly to the judgment because as Mr. Justice Brennan has suggested, that is what I must meet. I do say first about this business of notifying the unions. We did -- what was done in Street and they had a valid cause of action and our notification couldn't go beyond that as a factual matter, but now on the judgment, what does this judgment do? This judgment does not enjoin the union-shop generally at all. Here are the defects in the judgment that the Court found in Street, the Court said first it's defective because it's a class judgment. Here, there is no element of class judgment. It was rigorously eliminated by the trial court. Nobody purports to benefit from this judgment except people who are active litigants. What else did the Court say was wrong with the Street judgment? And I'm quoting now from Mr. Justice Brennan's opinion, page 18 of my brief, I will read just a sentence, “Restraining the collection of all funds, sweeps too broadly,” that on down, “the prohibition is absolutely against the collection of all funds. The complete shut off of this source of income defeats the congressional plan and so forth.” We also think that a blanket injunction against all expenditures of funds for the disputed purposes and so forth would not be proper. Now, Your Honors, the judgment before you does none of those things. This is not a judgment restraining the collection of all funds from these plaintiffs. It is not a prohibition absolute against the collection of all funds from these plaintiffs. There is no complete shut off of union funds from these plaintiffs and there's no injunction against expenditure of anything. In other words, under this judgment, the unions are left free to collect any and all amounts authorized by the union-shop agreement from voluntary employees and they are left free to collect any and all sums related to collective bargaining from all employees covered by the contract voluntary and dissenting alike.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible] -- I gather this is before the [Inaudible].

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: That's true but the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Surely.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: That's true, sir. That's part of the judgment. That, Mr. Union, if you will come and show what part is related to collective bargaining, what parts you use for political purposes, and you alone know, you alone have the facts and information necessary to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, do you think that is -- do you think that – is that [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Your Honor, all the phrases that I just quoted you which this -- which Your Honor mentioned as being the vice of the Street judgment, each and everyone is absent in this judgment. Now the dividing -- let me touch this. The dividing of what's related to collective bargaining and what's political, the unions in the first instance must do that. They only -- they alone know what they do with the money, they have the facts and the information as to how they use it, what part they use for political, what part they use for collective bargaining. They should make a showing on that in the first instance. As Mr. Justice Brennan writing in the case of United States-Campbell said, it is a general principle based in fairness that the law does not place upon a party the burden to proving facts which are peculiarly within the knowledge of the his adversary and our North Carolina decisions are positively to the same effect. And this dividing of what is related to bargaining and what isn't, were suggested by this Court in the United States against CIO which as Your Honors will remember was a case involving union publications for political purposes. And there, this Court said, if minority or dissent of protection is intended to be sufficient for securing this to permit the dissenting members to carry the burden of making known their position and to relieve them, relieve them of any duty to pay dues or portions of them to be applied to the forbidden uses. And the United States Government Industry case filed a brief in which it approved the same idea.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Mr. Blakeney, [Inaudible] it enjoins them on collective bargaining which is [Inaudible], isn't that the words of the injunction (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: As (Inaudible) -- as defined by the jury issues the judgment says.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Does the judgment -- however does not in its [Inaudible].

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No, it refers to the issues which do talk in terms of politics.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Now, looking to the records, I think that issue [Inaudible]. I figured that was [Inaudible] as you often have this problem, [Inaudible] based on contributions involving the party. And I'd like to ask if you whether -- do you regard that as the -- involves this injunction. This public [Inaudible] we're referring to is broad [Inaudible] --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible] under this injunction what have you, enjoining [Inaudible] is unrelated to collective bargaining.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I think it must be determined in closed cases, Your Honor. In difficult cases, it must be determined by a court. I know no other way to determine it whether that falls within what the jury decided here.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: What would you refer in the cases often [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: That some of it was indeed remote from collective bargaining. They spend it for TVA, juvenile delinquency, Hells Canyon, disclosure of inmate of FBI files, enemy alien assets, and pay television etcetera.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: And this would be if what I think [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: And would you [Inaudible] general characterization, the expenditures are not based under your [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Some of it certainly would, Your Honor. But political purposes and whatever is to be comprehended within that has got to be worked out of course in each case. All I am saying Your Honors is, that money here was used for political purposes undeniably to some degree. Now let the union show to what degree. The burden should be on them. Now, just one more word if the Court please, this Court, of course, has no solicitude or concern for the union-shop agreement insofar as it accomplishes as this Court itself held insofar as it accomplishes an unlawful purpose. That is the taking of money against an employee's will and using it for political purposes. What this Court was concerned about in the Street, as I understand it, and the reason this Court set the Street judgment back as I understand it was this. That in working the unlawful operation of the union-shop agreement, there must not be interference with the union-shop agreement functioning in its collective bargaining area, in its proper spheres. There, in other words, the Court considered that the judgment there perpetually enjoining, sweeping broadly, denying all collection of any nature was an interference with the bargaining function of the union. And therefore, sent that -- reversed that judgment and undid it. And I am saying Your Honor, that the judgment here touches and inhibits the union-shop agreement only in its unlawful sphere, and not at all in its other operations. Now suppo -- our adversaries, Your Honor, would apparently have these plaintiff employees bring suit against these unions, apparently which they term, if the Court please, sometimes, somewhere, someday by protected litigation, any expenditure of a thousand times what's involved, you may restrain the expenditure of some part of this or you make it restitution, but the compulsion, you must now submit to though it'd be unlawful. It's held unlawful, nevertheless submit to it. Pay all, now. This, I submit to the Court, it should not be. If a judgment is worked out so that the unlawful part is separated from the lawful part, then the unlawful part can and should be restrained. And if we have to sue every time Your Honor, that would be perpetual litigation. Each case would resolve nothing except the amount of money involved in that case only. And the employee in order to maintain the right of keeping his money from going for political purpose would have to sue again and again renewed, successively, perpetually to maintain that right. Now, this Court has said that this area is one of the utmost gravity. The issues underlying here are indeed important ones. They are vital, not only to individual liberty, if the Court please, they are crucial to the integrity of the Government itself for if arrangements are established by which collective representatives can require employees to pay money as a condition of working and can then take that money and elect whom they wish to public office and enact laws as they will, then we may be on the road toward distinction of important individual liberty and free government as well. It may -- it serves not, if the Court please, to declare this taking and using for political purposes to be unlawful. It avails not to declare it unlawful, if no meaningful, practical remedy is granted. And its purpose can still be accomplished and it can be still accomplished and will be accomplished unless injunction avails. And the injunction here, we do submit to Your Honors, is one that is shaped to avoid the very difficulties that this Court observed and pointed to in the Street case.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Did the Street say the exemption [Inaudible]

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: [Inaudible] reconsidered? Are you asking -- are you asking the Court now that we consider opposing about the remedy that should be provided that [Inaudible]? Would you then on that extreme?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: I am not, sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: How do you read the language [Inaudible] the court has said flatly, “We think that injunction was mainly enforcement [Inaudible] and therefore plainly not [Inaudible] appropriate in violation of the Act expressly on the Government. There was a [Inaudible] remedy.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: But here, Your Honor, we are not restraining the collection of all funds. What the Court was inveighing against was restraining the collection of who owe funds from these appellees. What the Court was inveighing against is the prohibition absolute against the collection of all funds. The complete shut off of income from these plaintiffs. We don't shut you. This judgment doesn't shut off income from these plaintiffs.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Wasn't the Court dealing with the -- restraining the enforcement of the union-shop agreement with plaintiffs' [Inaudible]?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No, sir. That's -- I wanted to advert to that for just a moment. Your Honor raised that earlier in Mr. -- with Mr. Kramer. Nothing except monetary payment is involved here at all. The Railway Labor Act, the statute, the union-shop agreement both have to do only with monetary payment compulsion, no other kind of compulsion. This is treated in a -- on the other last of my brief. They conceded in the Supreme Court of North Carolina in their brief, which I there refer to that they make no complaint about anything here and we make no complaint about anything here except the monetary matters. That's only compulsion involved.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If it -- does injunction run against the payment of dues by anyone except this individual people --

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- who had expressed the opposition to having their money used for this purpose.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Injunction runs in behalf of no one else.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: How many are they?

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
turns.text: Some 20 odd, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It doesn't run against all. It's not a blanket running against all.

turns.name: WHITEFORD S. BLAKENEY
